benchmark: Read grade-level text with fluency and comprehension, adjusting pace and expression based on purpose
item_specs: Students demonstrate fluent reading with appropriate pacing and expression
essential_questions: How does reading fluency affect comprehension?
Why is it important to adjust your reading pace?
learning_targets: I can read grade-level text fluently
I can adjust my pace based on the type of text
sample_assessment:
When reading aloud, a fluent reader should:
A) Read as fast as possible
B) Adjust pace and expression based on the text
C) Pause after every word
D) Read in a monotone voice

benchmark: Establish purpose for reading assigned and self-selected texts by analyzing the author's purpose and message within a text
item_specs: Students identify and analyze the author's purpose (inform, persuade, entertain, express)
essential_questions: Why did the author write this text?
How does knowing the author's purpose help you as a reader?
learning_targets: I can identify the author's purpose
I can set my own purpose for reading based on the text type
sample_assessment:
A newspaper editorial urging voters to support a school bond is written primarily to:
A) Entertain readers
B) Inform readers about school funding
C) Persuade readers to vote yes
D) Express the author's personal feelings

benchmark: Infer the theme of a literary text and distinguish theme from topic
item_specs: Students determine theme through textual evidence and distinguish it from the subject or topic
essential_questions: What is the difference between theme and topic?
How do you determine the theme of a story?
learning_targets: I can identify the theme of a literary text
I can explain the difference between theme and topic
sample_assessment:
A story is about a girl who overcomes her fear of public speaking to win a debate. The topic is public speaking. What is the theme?
A) Public speaking is difficult
B) Debate competitions are fun
C) Courage and perseverance lead to success
D) Girls are good at debating

benchmark: Analyze how the author's use of literary devices such as metaphor, simile, and personification contributes to meaning
item_specs: Students identify literary devices and explain how they enhance meaning
essential_questions: How do literary devices affect the reader's understanding?
Why do authors use figurative language?
learning_targets: I can identify metaphors, similes, and personification in text
I can explain how literary devices contribute to the meaning of a text
sample_assessment:
In the sentence 'The wind whispered through the trees,' what literary device is used?
A) Simile
B) Metaphor
C) Personification
D) Alliteration

benchmark: Analyze the author's use of print and graphic features to achieve specific purposes in informational texts
item_specs: Students evaluate how text features (headings, charts, diagrams) support comprehension
essential_questions: How do text features help you understand informational text?
Why do authors include graphic features?
learning_targets: I can identify and explain the purpose of text features
I can use text features to locate and understand information
sample_assessment:
An author includes a bar graph in a science article about water usage. The purpose is most likely to:
A) Make the article longer
B) Show data visually so readers can compare amounts
C) Decorate the page
D) Replace the written explanation

benchmark: Compose literary texts such as personal narratives, fiction, and poetry using genre characteristics and craft
item_specs: Students write original literary texts incorporating appropriate genre elements
essential_questions: What elements make a strong narrative?
How do you use genre characteristics to shape your writing?
learning_targets: I can write a personal narrative with a clear beginning, middle, and end
I can use genre characteristics in my creative writing
sample_assessment:
Which element is essential for a personal narrative?
A) A bibliography
B) A real experience told from the author's perspective
C) Rhyming couplets
D) A persuasive thesis statement

benchmark: Compose informational texts, including multi-paragraph essays that convey information about a topic, using a clear controlling idea supported by evidence
item_specs: Students write organized informational essays with thesis, evidence, and analysis
essential_questions: How do you organize an informational essay?
What makes evidence effective in supporting a controlling idea?
learning_targets: I can write a multi-paragraph essay with a controlling idea
I can support my ideas with relevant evidence
sample_assessment:
What is the role of a controlling idea in an informational essay?
A) To provide background information
B) To state the main point the essay will develop
C) To summarize the conclusion
D) To list the sources used

benchmark: Plan a first draft by selecting a genre appropriate for a particular topic, purpose, and audience using a range of strategies such as brainstorming and mapping
item_specs: Students demonstrate effective planning strategies before drafting
essential_questions: Why is planning important before writing?
How do you choose the right genre for your purpose?
learning_targets: I can select an appropriate genre for my writing task
I can use prewriting strategies to organize my ideas
sample_assessment:
A student wants to convince the principal to allow longer lunch periods. Which genre is most appropriate?
A) A short story
B) A poem
C) A persuasive letter
D) A personal narrative

benchmark: Revise drafts for clarity, development, organization, style, word choice, and sentence variety
item_specs: Students make meaningful revisions to improve content, organization, and style
essential_questions: How is revising different from editing?
What should you focus on when revising?
learning_targets: I can revise my writing for clarity and organization
I can improve word choice and sentence variety in my drafts
sample_assessment:
Which revision improves sentence variety?
Original: 'The dog ran. The dog jumped. The dog barked.'
A) The dog ran, jumped, and barked.
B) The dog ran. The dog jumped. The dog barked loudly.
C) Running, jumping, and barking, the dog played in the yard.
D) Both A and C would improve sentence variety.

benchmark: Determine the meaning of grade-level academic English words derived from Latin, Greek, and other linguistic roots and affixes
item_specs: Students use knowledge of roots and affixes to determine word meaning
essential_questions: How do roots and affixes help you determine word meaning?
Why is it helpful to know Latin and Greek roots?
learning_targets: I can use Latin and Greek roots to determine word meanings
I can use prefixes and suffixes to figure out unfamiliar words
sample_assessment:
The Latin root 'bene' means 'good.' What does 'beneficial' most likely mean?
A) Harmful
B) Helpful or advantageous
C) Beautiful
D) Brave

benchmark: Read grade-level text with fluency and comprehension, making adjustments when understanding breaks down
item_specs: Students monitor comprehension and use fix-up strategies when reading
essential_questions: What do good readers do when they don't understand?
How do you know when comprehension breaks down?
learning_targets: I can monitor my understanding while reading
I can use strategies to fix comprehension problems
sample_assessment:
When a reader encounters an unfamiliar word and loses understanding, the best strategy is to:
A) Skip the word and keep reading
B) Stop reading entirely
C) Use context clues and word parts to determine meaning
D) Ask someone else to read it

benchmark: Analyze how the author's choices concerning how to structure a text create effects such as mystery, tension, or surprise
item_specs: Students analyze structural choices and their effects on reader experience
essential_questions: How does text structure affect the reader's experience?
Why do authors choose specific structures for their writing?
learning_targets: I can analyze how text structure creates effects like suspense or surprise
I can explain why an author chose a specific structure
sample_assessment:
An author begins a mystery novel with the crime scene before going back to show events leading up to it. This structure creates:
A) Comedy
B) Suspense and curiosity
C) Confusion
D) A happy ending

benchmark: Analyze the development of the central or key idea in informational text, including its relationship to supporting ideas
item_specs: Students trace how central ideas develop across a text through supporting details
essential_questions: How does an author develop the central idea throughout a text?
What is the relationship between central ideas and supporting details?
learning_targets: I can identify the central idea of an informational text
I can explain how supporting details develop the central idea
sample_assessment:
In an article about endangered species, the central idea is that habitat loss is the primary threat. Which detail best supports this idea?
A) Some animals are very colorful
B) Scientists study animals in labs
C) Deforestation destroys 18 million acres of forest each year
D) Zoos help educate the public

benchmark: Write argumentative texts to influence the attitudes or actions of a specific audience on specific issues, using relevant evidence and logical reasoning
item_specs: Students compose arguments with claims, evidence, reasoning, and counterarguments
essential_questions: What makes an argument convincing?
How do you use evidence to support a claim?
learning_targets: I can write an argumentative essay with a clear claim
I can support my argument with relevant evidence and reasoning
sample_assessment:
Which is the strongest piece of evidence to support the claim that school should start later?
A) I feel tired in the morning
B) Studies show teens who start school after 8:30 AM have higher grades
C) Some of my friends agree with me
D) Other countries have different school schedules

benchmark: Use context clues, including contrast, cause and effect, and comparisons, to determine the meaning of words and phrases
item_specs: Students use various types of context clues to determine word meaning
essential_questions: How can context help you figure out unfamiliar words?
What types of context clues are most helpful?
learning_targets: I can use context clues to determine word meaning
I can identify different types of context clues
sample_assessment:
In the sentence 'Unlike her gregarious sister, Maya was shy and reserved,' the word 'gregarious' most likely means:
A) Shy
B) Outgoing and sociable
C) Angry
D) Intelligent

benchmark: Analyze the author's purpose and message within a text using text evidence to support the analysis
item_specs: Students support their analysis of author's purpose with specific text evidence
essential_questions: How do you determine the author's message?
What text evidence supports your analysis of purpose?
learning_targets: I can analyze the author's purpose using text evidence
I can distinguish between stated and implied messages
sample_assessment:
An author writes a detailed account of working conditions in factories in the early 1900s, including children's stories. The author's purpose is most likely to:
A) Entertain with exciting factory stories
B) Inform readers and persuade them that child labor was wrong
C) Describe factory architecture
D) Teach readers how factories work

benchmark: Analyze how authors use key literary elements, including setting, plot, and characterization, to contribute to meaning in literary texts
item_specs: Students analyze how literary elements work together to develop meaning
essential_questions: How do setting, plot, and characters work together to create meaning?
How does characterization reveal theme?
learning_targets: I can analyze how setting affects plot and character
I can explain how characterization contributes to the theme
sample_assessment:
In a story set during the Texas Revolution, a young farmer must choose between staying to protect his land or joining the fight for independence. The setting most contributes to the story by:
A) Providing a peaceful backdrop
B) Creating the central conflict the character faces
C) Making the story funnier
D) Introducing the resolution

benchmark: Analyze the effect of metrics, rhyme schemes, and other conventions in poems across a variety of poetic forms
item_specs: Students identify and analyze poetic conventions and their effects across different forms
essential_questions: How do meter and rhyme scheme affect a poem's meaning and mood?
What makes different poetic forms unique?
learning_targets: I can identify meter and rhyme scheme in poetry
I can analyze how poetic conventions contribute to meaning
sample_assessment:
A poem with the rhyme scheme ABAB CDCD EFEF GG is most likely a:
A) Haiku
B) Limerick
C) Shakespearean sonnet
D) Free verse poem

benchmark: Generate and clarify research questions on a topic and develop a plan for answering them
item_specs: Students formulate research questions and create research plans
essential_questions: What makes a good research question?
How do you develop a plan for research?
learning_targets: I can generate focused research questions
I can create a research plan with appropriate sources
sample_assessment:
Which is the best research question about the Alamo?
A) What is the Alamo?
B) How did the Battle of the Alamo influence the outcome of the Texas Revolution?
C) Is the Alamo in San Antonio?
D) Do people visit the Alamo?

benchmark: Write multi-paragraph argumentative texts using an organizing structure appropriate to purpose, audience, and task
item_specs: Students write well-organized argumentative essays with introduction, body, and conclusion
essential_questions: How do you organize an argumentative essay effectively?
How does structure strengthen an argument?
learning_targets: I can organize an argumentative essay with a clear structure
I can address counterarguments in my writing
sample_assessment:
In an argumentative essay, where should the writer address the opposing viewpoint?
A) In the introduction only
B) In the conclusion only
C) In the body paragraphs, before or after presenting their own evidence
D) It should never be addressed

benchmark: Analyze the impact of diction and syntax on the tone and mood of a text
item_specs: Students analyze how word choice and sentence structure create tone and mood
essential_questions: How does word choice affect the tone of writing?
What is the relationship between syntax and mood?
learning_targets: I can analyze how diction contributes to tone
I can explain how syntax creates mood in a text
sample_assessment:
An author uses short, choppy sentences and harsh-sounding words to describe a battle scene. This creates a mood that is:
A) Calm and peaceful
B) Tense and urgent
C) Humorous and light
D) Sad and reflective

benchmark: Analyze how complex characters develop over the course of a text, interact with other characters, and advance the plot or develop the theme
item_specs: Students trace character development and its relationship to plot and theme
essential_questions: How do characters change over the course of a text?
How does character development relate to theme?
learning_targets: I can trace a character's development throughout a text
I can explain how character interactions advance the plot
sample_assessment:
A character who starts the novel as selfish but learns to sacrifice for others is best described as:
A) A static character
B) A flat character
C) A dynamic character
D) An antagonist

benchmark: Evaluate the author's use of rhetorical devices and logical fallacies in argumentative texts
item_specs: Students identify and evaluate rhetorical strategies and reasoning errors
essential_questions: How do rhetorical devices strengthen or weaken an argument?
What are common logical fallacies?
learning_targets: I can identify rhetorical devices in argumentative texts
I can recognize common logical fallacies
sample_assessment:
A politician says, 'My opponent can't be trusted on education policy because he failed math in high school.' This is an example of:
A) Logos
B) Ethos
C) Ad hominem fallacy
D) Straw man fallacy

benchmark: Write literary analysis essays that analyze how authors use literary elements and techniques to convey meaning
item_specs: Students compose analytical essays examining literary elements and their effects
essential_questions: How do you write an effective literary analysis?
What evidence from the text supports your analysis?
learning_targets: I can write a literary analysis essay with a clear thesis
I can use textual evidence to support my analysis
sample_assessment:
Which thesis best demonstrates literary analysis?
A) I liked the novel because it was interesting
B) Shakespeare uses light and dark imagery in Romeo and Juliet to emphasize the contrast between love and conflict
C) Romeo and Juliet is a play by Shakespeare
D) The book has many characters

benchmark: Analyze how an author's choices about structure create specific effects and contribute to meaning and aesthetic impact
item_specs: Students analyze structural choices in literary and informational texts and evaluate their effects
essential_questions: How do structural choices affect meaning?
What is the aesthetic impact of an author's structural decisions?
learning_targets: I can analyze how an author's structural choices create effects
I can evaluate the aesthetic impact of text structure
sample_assessment:
An author alternates between two timelines throughout a novel. This structural choice most likely serves to:
A) Confuse the reader
B) Create parallels and reveal how past events influence the present
C) Make the book longer
D) Show the author is creative

benchmark: Analyze seminal U.S. and world documents of historical and literary significance for their themes, purposes, and rhetorical features
item_specs: Students analyze foundational documents for rhetorical strategies and historical significance
essential_questions: How do seminal documents reflect the values of their time?
What rhetorical strategies make these documents effective?
learning_targets: I can analyze the rhetorical features of historical documents
I can explain the significance of seminal documents
sample_assessment:
The Declaration of Independence states that all men are 'endowed by their Creator with certain unalienable Rights.' The rhetorical purpose of this phrase is to:
A) Describe a religious ceremony
B) Establish that these rights come from a higher authority, not the government
C) List specific laws
D) Entertain the reader

benchmark: Write argumentative texts to influence the attitudes or actions of a specific audience on specific issues using valid reasoning and sufficient evidence
item_specs: Students write arguments using logical reasoning, credible evidence, and clear organization
essential_questions: What makes an argument logically valid?
How do you select the most convincing evidence?
learning_targets: I can write an argument using valid reasoning
I can select and integrate sufficient evidence to support my claims
sample_assessment:
Which type of evidence would be most credible in an argument about climate change?
A) A social media post
B) A personal opinion
C) Peer-reviewed scientific research
D) A fictional story about weather

benchmark: Analyze works of American literature from multiple genres representing the diversity of American culture and experience
item_specs: Students read and analyze American literary works across genres and cultural perspectives
essential_questions: How does literature reflect the diversity of the American experience?
What themes connect different works of American literature?
learning_targets: I can analyze works of American literature across genres
I can identify how literature reflects diverse American experiences
sample_assessment:
Which pair of works best represents the diversity of American literary perspectives?
A) Two novels by the same author
B) The Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass and The Great Gatsby
C) Two textbook chapters about literature
D) A dictionary and a thesaurus

benchmark: Evaluate the structure and elements of argumentative and informational texts, analyzing how the organization supports the purpose
item_specs: Students evaluate how organizational structure supports the author's purpose in nonfiction texts
essential_questions: How does organizational structure support an author's argument?
What makes an informational text effective?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the structure of argumentative texts
I can analyze how organization supports the author's purpose
sample_assessment:
An author places the strongest piece of evidence last in an argumentative essay. This organizational choice is most effective because:
A) It fills space at the end
B) It leaves the reader with the most compelling evidence fresh in mind
C) Weak evidence should always come first
D) It is the only correct structure

benchmark: Write analytical essays that develop a thesis based on textual evidence and demonstrate an understanding of rhetorical strategies
item_specs: Students write analytical essays with strong theses, integrated evidence, and sophisticated analysis
essential_questions: How do you develop a strong analytical thesis?
What makes textual evidence effective in an essay?
learning_targets: I can develop a strong thesis based on textual evidence
I can demonstrate understanding of rhetorical strategies in my analysis
sample_assessment:
Which demonstrates the best integration of textual evidence?
A) The author says some stuff about freedom.
B) According to the text, 'freedom is important.'
C) The author's repeated use of the word 'liberty' in paragraphs 3-5 emphasizes the colonists' central motivation for revolution.
D) Quote: 'liberty'

benchmark: Analyze how authors from diverse backgrounds use literary elements and rhetorical devices to explore universal themes in world literature
item_specs: Students analyze world literature for universal themes and culturally specific elements
essential_questions: What themes are universal across cultures?
How do authors from different cultures approach similar themes?
learning_targets: I can identify universal themes across world literature
I can analyze how cultural context shapes literary expression
sample_assessment:
The theme of 'the hero's journey' appears in the Epic of Gilgamesh, the Odyssey, and modern novels. This demonstrates that:
A) All authors copy each other
B) Certain themes are universal across cultures and time periods
C) Only ancient literature has themes
D) Heroes only exist in fiction

benchmark: Synthesize information from multiple sources to create new understanding and evaluate the credibility and relevance of sources
item_specs: Students synthesize information from various sources and evaluate source quality
essential_questions: How do you synthesize information from multiple sources?
What criteria determine a source's credibility?
learning_targets: I can synthesize information from multiple sources
I can evaluate the credibility and relevance of sources
sample_assessment:
When researching the effects of social media on teens, which source is most credible?
A) A teenager's blog post
B) A peer-reviewed study published in a psychology journal
C) A social media company's advertisement
D) A Wikipedia article

benchmark: Edit drafts using standard English conventions, including correct use of subject-verb agreement, pronoun-antecedent agreement, and consistent verb tense
item_specs: Students identify and correct errors in grammar and usage
essential_questions: Why are grammar conventions important in writing?
How do you edit for correct grammar?
learning_targets: I can edit for subject-verb agreement
I can maintain consistent verb tense in my writing
sample_assessment:
Which sentence demonstrates correct subject-verb agreement?
A) The group of students are working on their project.
B) The group of students is working on their project.
C) The group of students is working on its project.
D) Both B and C are correct.

benchmark: Evaluate the credibility of a source, including determining its relevance and reliability, and citing textual evidence to support analysis
item_specs: Students evaluate sources for credibility and use textual evidence in analysis
essential_questions: How do you determine if a source is credible?
What makes textual evidence strong?
learning_targets: I can evaluate the credibility of a source
I can cite textual evidence to support my analysis
sample_assessment:
Which factor is LEAST important when evaluating a source's credibility?
A) The author's qualifications
B) The date of publication
C) The length of the article
D) Whether the source shows bias

benchmark: Analyze the use of analogy, allusion, and extended metaphor in a variety of literary and informational texts
item_specs: Students identify and analyze the effects of analogy, allusion, and extended metaphor
essential_questions: How do analogy, allusion, and extended metaphor enhance meaning?
Why do authors use references to other works?
learning_targets: I can identify analogies, allusions, and extended metaphors in text
I can explain how these devices contribute to meaning
sample_assessment:
A speech compares the struggle for civil rights to the long journey of the Israelites out of Egypt. This is an example of:
A) Simile
B) Allusion
C) Hyperbole
D) Onomatopoeia

benchmark: Conduct research to answer a question, drawing on multiple authoritative sources and assessing the credibility of each source
item_specs: Students conduct multi-source research and evaluate source reliability
essential_questions: How do you select authoritative sources for research?
What makes a research question effective?
learning_targets: I can conduct research using multiple authoritative sources
I can assess the credibility and relevance of each source
sample_assessment:
A student researching the Battle of San Jacinto should consider which source most authoritative?
A) A historical fiction novel set during the battle
B) A peer-reviewed article in a Texas history journal
C) A blog post written by a tourist who visited the site
D) A social media post about Texas history

benchmark: Use the conventions of academic language, including discipline-specific terminology, when writing for formal audiences
item_specs: Students demonstrate command of academic language conventions in formal writing
essential_questions: How does academic language differ from informal language?
Why is discipline-specific vocabulary important?
learning_targets: I can use academic language appropriate for formal writing
I can incorporate discipline-specific vocabulary correctly
sample_assessment:
Which sentence uses the most appropriate academic language for a formal essay?
A) The experiment was really cool and showed some neat results.
B) The experimental data demonstrated a statistically significant correlation between the variables.
C) The science thing worked and stuff happened.
D) I thought the experiment was pretty interesting.

benchmark: Use print or digital resources to determine the meaning, syllabication, pronunciation, and word origin of unknown words
item_specs: Students use reference materials to find word information
essential_questions: How do reference materials help you learn new words?
What information can you find in a dictionary entry?
learning_targets: I can use a dictionary to find word meanings and pronunciations
I can use word origins to understand meaning
sample_assessment:
Which part of a dictionary entry tells you where a word originally came from?
A) The pronunciation guide
B) The definition
C) The etymology
D) The part of speech

benchmark: Compose multi-paragraph texts that include a clear thesis or controlling idea, organized with transitions and supported by evidence
item_specs: Students write organized multi-paragraph texts with clear structure
essential_questions: How do you organize a multi-paragraph essay?
What role do transitions play in writing?
learning_targets: I can write a multi-paragraph essay with a clear thesis
I can use transitions to connect ideas between paragraphs
sample_assessment:
Which transition word best connects two contrasting ideas?
A) Furthermore
B) However
C) Additionally
D) Similarly

benchmark: Write responses to literary or expository texts that demonstrate understanding of the text and provide evidence from the text to support the interpretation
item_specs: Students write analytical responses using textual evidence
essential_questions: How do you support your interpretation with text evidence?
What makes a strong text-based response?
learning_targets: I can write a response that demonstrates understanding of a text
I can use text evidence to support my interpretation
sample_assessment:
When writing a text-based response, the strongest evidence comes from:
A) Your personal opinions
B) Direct quotes or specific details from the text
C) Information from other books
D) What your classmates said about the text

benchmark: Spell correctly, including using various resources to determine and check correct spellings, and use punctuation marks correctly including semicolons, colons, and hyphens
item_specs: Students demonstrate correct spelling and punctuation usage
essential_questions: When do you use a semicolon versus a comma?
How do colons and hyphens function in sentences?
learning_targets: I can use semicolons, colons, and hyphens correctly
I can spell grade-level words correctly
sample_assessment:
Which sentence correctly uses a semicolon?
A) I like reading; and writing.
B) I like reading; writing is fun too.
C) I like; reading and writing.
D) I like reading and; writing.

benchmark: Analyze how different points of view or cultural experiences are reflected in works of literature from various periods and cultures
item_specs: Students analyze how cultural context shapes literary works
essential_questions: How does an author's cultural background influence their writing?
How do different points of view shape a narrative?
learning_targets: I can analyze how point of view affects a narrative
I can identify how cultural experiences are reflected in literature
sample_assessment:
A novel written from the perspective of an enslaved person in the 1800s reflects:
A) Only the author's imagination
B) The cultural and historical experiences of that time period
C) Modern political views
D) Universal experiences with no cultural influence

benchmark: Analyze the use of satire, sarcasm, irony, and understatement in literary and informational texts
item_specs: Students identify and analyze the effects of satirical and ironic devices
essential_questions: How do authors use irony and satire to convey meaning?
What is the difference between verbal, situational, and dramatic irony?
learning_targets: I can identify different types of irony in text
I can analyze how satire conveys social commentary
sample_assessment:
In a story, a fire station burns down. This is an example of:
A) Verbal irony
B) Dramatic irony
C) Situational irony
D) Sarcasm

benchmark: Edit writing for correct use of the conventions of standard English, including complex sentence structures, parallel structure, and correct use of who/whom
item_specs: Students edit for advanced grammar and usage conventions
essential_questions: What is parallel structure and why is it important?
How do you determine when to use who vs. whom?
learning_targets: I can edit for parallel structure in my writing
I can correctly use who and whom
sample_assessment:
Which sentence demonstrates correct parallel structure?
A) She likes swimming, to run, and biking.
B) She likes swimming, running, and biking.
C) She likes to swim, running, and to bike.
D) She likes swim, run, and bike.

benchmark: Write clear, coherent research papers that marshal evidence in support of a clear thesis and anticipate and address the audience's concerns and counterarguments
item_specs: Students write research papers with thesis, evidence, and counterargument consideration
essential_questions: How do you structure a research paper effectively?
Why is addressing counterarguments important in research writing?
learning_targets: I can write a research paper with a clear thesis supported by evidence
I can anticipate and address counterarguments
sample_assessment:
Addressing counterarguments in a research paper strengthens your argument because it:
A) Makes the paper longer
B) Shows you have considered multiple perspectives and can defend your position
C) Proves the opposition is wrong
D) Is required for a passing grade

benchmark: Analyze how the author's use of language creates voice and contributes to the overall meaning and tone of a text
item_specs: Students analyze how language choices create a distinct authorial voice
essential_questions: How does an author's language create a unique voice?
What is the relationship between voice and tone?
learning_targets: I can analyze how language choices create voice
I can explain how voice contributes to meaning and tone
sample_assessment:
An author writes with short, clipped sentences and informal slang throughout a novel. This creates a voice that is:
A) Formal and academic
B) Casual and direct
C) Poetic and lyrical
D) Objective and detached

benchmark: Analyze how the use of text structure contributes to the author's purpose in informational texts, including compare/contrast, cause/effect, and problem/solution
item_specs: Students identify organizational patterns and explain how they support the author's purpose
essential_questions: How does text structure help convey the author's message?
What are the common organizational patterns in informational text?
learning_targets: I can identify text structures including compare/contrast, cause/effect, and problem/solution
I can explain how text structure supports the author's purpose
sample_assessment:
An article discusses the effects of deforestation on wildlife habitats and then proposes reforestation programs. This text structure is:
A) Compare and contrast
B) Chronological order
C) Problem and solution
D) Cause and effect only

benchmark: Explain how dramatic conventions and devices, such as soliloquy, aside, and dramatic irony, function in drama
item_specs: Students identify and explain the function of dramatic conventions in plays
essential_questions: What are the unique conventions of drama?
How do dramatic devices enhance storytelling?
learning_targets: I can identify dramatic conventions such as soliloquy and aside
I can explain how dramatic irony creates tension for the audience
sample_assessment:
When a character in a play speaks directly to the audience while other characters cannot hear, this is called:
A) A soliloquy
B) An aside
C) A monologue
D) Dialogue

benchmark: Analyze the effects of sound devices in poetry, including alliteration, assonance, consonance, and onomatopoeia
item_specs: Students identify sound devices and analyze their effects on meaning and mood
essential_questions: How do sound devices enhance poetry?
What effect does alliteration have on the reader?
learning_targets: I can identify sound devices in poetry
I can analyze how sound devices contribute to mood and meaning
sample_assessment:
In the line 'The silken, sad, uncertain rustling of each purple curtain,' which sound device is most prominent?
A) Onomatopoeia
B) Alliteration of the 's' sound
C) Rhyme
D) Hyperbole

benchmark: Evaluate the use of both print and digital media to present information, analyzing how different media shape the audience's perception of the topic
item_specs: Students evaluate how different media formats affect the presentation and perception of information
essential_questions: How does the medium affect the message?
How do print and digital media shape perception differently?
learning_targets: I can evaluate how different media present information
I can analyze how media choices shape audience perception
sample_assessment:
A news story presented as a video with dramatic music and images of destruction will most likely create a perception that is:
A) Identical to a print article on the same topic
B) More emotional and urgent than the same story in print
C) More objective than print
D) Less effective than print
